title: apkallu_attestations data payload
description:
Structured payload for the `apkallu_attestations` tool — surfaces named occurrences of the seven antediluvian apkallū (and the postdiluvian successor-sages) across the cuneiform and Hellenistic textual record.

Returns: per-sage attestations grouped by source-text (Bīt Mēseri, Uruk List of Kings and Sages, Berossus, etc.); iconographic forms where attested (fish-cloaked, bird-headed, figurine); the antediluvian king each sage is paired with in the standard tradition; cross-reference to the postdiluvian *ummânū* successor in the Uruk List.

Provenance is `source: local`, `endpoint: local:apkalluAttestationIndex`. Underlying data is curated from Reiner 1961 (Bīt Mēseri), Lenzi 2008 (full scribal tradition), Annus 2010 (comparative), Verderame 2013 (iconography), plus the Research/Apkallu.md brief.

query.description: Echo of the input query, normalized.

sage_name.description: Specific sage queried (e.g. `Uanna`, `Adapa`, `Enmeduga`). If absent, returns all seven canonical sages plus the four postdiluvian successors.

include_iconography.description: If true (default), each attestation carries iconographic form data where the source is a visual artifact rather than a text.

include_postdiluvian.description: If true (default true), include the four named *ummânū* successors from the Uruk List (Nungalpirigal, Piriggalnungal, Piriggalabsu, Lu-Nanna). If false, antediluvian seven only.

sages.description: One entry per sage, in canonical order (Uanna first; postdiluvian successors after the seventh antediluvian if included).

name.description: Canonical Akkadian-form name. E.g. `Uanna` (= Oannes in Berossus), `Uanduga`, `Enmeduga`, `Enmegalama`, `Enmebulugga`, `An-Enlilda`, `Utuabzu`. For postdiluvian: `Nungalpirigal`, `Piriggalnungal`, `Piriggalabsu`, `Lu-Nanna`.

alt_names.description: Variants across sources. E.g. for Uanna: `["U-An", "U'an", "Oannes (Berossus)"]`.

tier.description: `antediluvian` for the canonical seven; `postdiluvian` for the four named successor *ummânū*; `collective` for the broader unattributed visual tradition where the iconographic type (fish-cloaked, bird-headed, human-form) is identifiable but no specific named sage attribution exists.

paired_king.description: Antediluvian king this sage is paired with in the Uruk List of Kings and Sages. E.g. Uanna ~ Ayalu (Alulim), Uanduga ~ Alalgar. Empty/absent for postdiluvian sages.

discipline_specialization.description: Disciplines associated with this sage where attested. Drawn from the canon of apkallu-transmitted knowledge: `writing`, `lexicography`, `celestial_divination`, `terrestrial_divination`, `exorcism`, `medicine`, `hepatoscopy`, `mathematical_astronomy`, `lamentation_priest_craft`. Often empty — most sages are not specifically disciplinarily marked except in late tradition.

attestations.description: Where this sage is named or depicted. One entry per source-text or artifact.

source.description: Source text or artifact. Textual examples: `Bīt Mēseri III`, `Uruk List of Kings and Sages (W.20030,7)`, `Berossus, Babyloniaca (Syncellus excerpt)`, `LKA 76`, `KAR 142`. Artifact examples: `Nimrud relief slab BM 124577`, `Aššur figurine deposit VA 8200`.

source_type.description: Kind of attestation. Textual types in upper enum; artifact types lower.

citation.description: Line range, tablet section, or museum number. E.g. `Bīt Mēseri III.lines 1-21`, `W.20030,7 obv. col.i.6-10`, `BM 124577`.

approximate_date.description: Approximate composition or production date. E.g. `Neo-Assyrian (c. 700 BCE)`, `Seleucid (c. 165 BCE)`, `Hellenistic (c. 280 BCE)`.

iconography.description: Present for visual-artifact attestations. Describes the visual form.

scholarly_anchor.description: Modern critical edition or study. E.g. `Reiner 1961, p.4-6` for Bīt Mēseri III. `Lenzi 2008, p.106-109` for Uruk List.

secondary_anchor.description: Additional load-bearing modern reference where one exists. E.g. for Utuabzu's heaven-ascent: `Borger 1974, JNES 33:183-196 — established the Enoch parallel`. Optional.

excerpt.description: Translation excerpt of the relevant passage. Length capped at ~6 lines. Present only for textual attestations.

epithet.description: Specific epithet attached to the sage in this attestation. E.g. for Uanna in Bīt Mēseri III: `who accomplishes the plans of heaven and earth`. Optional but high-value for the canonical Bīt Mēseri III attestations where the formula-epithets are diagnostic.

tablet_position.description: Structural position within the source-text. E.g. `Tablet III, lines 1-7 (sage 1 of 7)`, `Tablet III, lines 22-28 (postdiluvian sage 1 of 4)`. Optional — useful for queryability of position-indexed corpora like Bīt Mēseri.

form.description: Canonical iconographic form. `fish_cloaked` = the Oannes-type figure draped in a fish-skin; `bird_headed_griffin` = eagle-headed winged genie (Apkallu-Nisroch type); `human_form` = unambiguously human-figured sage; `figurine` = small ritual figurine deposit (clay or wood).

wiggermann_type.description: Wiggermann 1992's taxonomic categorization. `puradu` = fish-cloaked (= Oannes-form); `bird_apkallu` = bird-headed winged genie; `umu_apkallu` = the human-formed, often-winged variant typical of palace doorway and throne-room reliefs. Optional — when present, formalizes the alignment with the foundational iconographic study.

ritual_function.description: Functional context of the depiction. `apotropaic` = active warding; `protective_deposit` = buried/walled-in protective figurines; `narrative_decoration` = panel relief depicting the figure as part of a scene; `unspecified` when context is lost.

location_in_situ.description: Original architectural or archaeological context where known. E.g. `Northwest Palace, Nimrud, Throneroom B reliefs`, `foundation deposit under threshold, Aššur`.

palace_room.description: Specific palace room or panel where attested. E.g. `Throne Room B, panel 6`, `Room G, panel 23`. Optional — adds queryability for palace-context research.

bucket_and_cone_present.description: Whether the figure depicted holds the canonical bucket (banduddû) and date-spathe cone (mullilu) gesture. The single most-attested ritual element of the apkallu visual tradition. Optional — when present, surfaces this iconographic feature for queryable research.
